2026-04-21T10:43:41.0816214Z I have enough information for the analysis. Let me look at the exact diff needed to fix the eventhub failures:
2026-04-21T10:43:41.0817081Z 
2026-04-21T10:43:42.0357914Z ● Read errors.ts
2026-04-21T10:43:42.0358338Z   │ sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts
2026-04-21T10:43:42.0358901Z   └ L676:698 (23 lines read)
2026-04-21T10:43:42.0359064Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:16.7334880Z ● create_issue (MCP: safeoutputs) · title: "CI test failures on main: @azure/event-hubs", body: "##…
2026-04-21T10:44:16.7335797Z   └ {"result":"success"}
2026-04-21T10:44:16.7336392Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:19.3094969Z **Summary:**
2026-04-21T10:44:19.3095229Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:20.5175352Z Found **1 package** with real unit test failures on the CI (playback) pipeline: **`@azure/event-hubs`** with 9 recurring failing tests.
2026-04-21T10:44:20.5176517Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:22.9188642Z **Root cause:** In Node.js 22+, DNS resolution failures are returned as `AggregateError` (combining multiple lookup failures) rather than a plain system error. The `translate()` function in `@azure/core-amqp` doesn't handle `AggregateError`, so it passes through untranslated instead of being wrapped in a `MessagingError`. Tests that connect to a non-existent namespace (`sb://a`) expect a `MessagingError` with `code: "ENOTFOUND"` but receive the raw `AggregateError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:22.9190379Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:22.9792530Z **Suggested fix:** Add an `AggregateError` guard in `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts#translate()` to recursively translate the first inner error.
2026-04-21T10:44:22.9793820Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:24.9308425Z All other CI failures (browser runner exits, missing artifact paths, OIDC token timeouts, broken external links) had **0 test failures** and are infrastructure-only.

2026-04-21T10:44:28.8529961Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8530392Z   Let me analyze the eventhub failures based on the code alone, then try to see if I can understand the root cause from the code.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8530897Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8531576Z   The test expects that when connecting to a non-existent namespace (sb://a), the error should be a `MessagingError` with code `ENOTFOUND` (on Node). But instead, it's getting an `AggregateError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8532317Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8532480Z   This is likely because:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8532960Z   1. Node.js DNS lookup for an invalid hostname now returns an `AggregateError` aggregating multiple DNS resolution failures
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8533466Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8533611Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8534492Z   2. The underlying AMQP libraries may have changed how they propagate connection errors, or the core-amqp error handling needs to be updated to recognize and properly wrap `AggregateError` instances as `MessagingError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8535336Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8535656Z   Let me check the core-amqp error handling to see how it's currently wrapping these errors.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8536190Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8536858Z ✓ $ grep -rn "AggregateError\|translateError\|ENOTFOUND\|ServiceCommunicationError" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/ | head -30
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8537497Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8537763Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [225, 295])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8538072Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8539215Z ◆ Now I understand! The `retry` function in `core-amqp` has been updated to throw an `AggregateError` when there are multiple errors accumulated during retries. When there is only 1 error, it throws that error directly. When there are multiple errors (from multiple retry attempts), it throws an `AggregateError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8540379Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8540541Z   So the failing test is:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8540812Z   1. Connecting to a non-existent namespace (sb://a) 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8541218Z   2. This causes a connection error -> `MessagingError` with code `ENOTFOUND`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8541809Z   3. If the retry mechanism retries the operation, it accumulates multiple errors and throws an `AggregateError`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8542272Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8542416Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8542881Z   4. The test expects the `MessagingError` directly but instead receives an `AggregateError` wrapping it, so the instanceof check fails.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8543427Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8544224Z   The test is looking for a `MessagingError` with a specific code property, but now it's getting an `AggregateError` containing that error instead. I should check the git history of the retry logic to understand when this behavior changed.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8545038Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8545504Z ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js && git log --no-pager --oneline -10 -- sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8546355Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8546835Z ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js && git log --oneline -10 -- sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts 2>&1 | head -20
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8547309Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8547864Z ◆ Hmm, the git log shows only one commit for retry.ts, but this repo doesn't have a full history. Let me check the current state of the retry function more carefully.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8548480Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8548917Z   Let me look at what the `translate` function does with `AggregateError` to understand whether the issue is in the translation:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8549369Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8549856Z ✓ $ grep -n "AggregateError\|translate\b" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts | head -30
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8550356Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8550624Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [672, 760])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8550940Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8551721Z ◆ I see that the `translate` function doesn't handle `AggregateError`. If an `AggregateError` is passed to it, it would just return it as-is (since it's a plain `Error`).The issue is in `retry.ts` at line 284:```typescript
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8554210Z   throw new AggregateError(errors);```This throws an `AggregateError` when there are multiple errors (from multiple retry attempts). The test expects a `MessagingError` to be thrown directly, but when the operation retries and then fails multiple times, it gets an `AggregateError`.Let me look at the retry configuration used in the test - what's the retry count for `EventHubConsumerClient`?Actually, let me look at what happens when connecting to a non-existent namespace. With `ENOTFOUND` error on Node.js, is this retryable?Looking at the code:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8556323Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8556476Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8557395Z   Now I need to trace through the error mapping chain: `ENOTFOUND` gets converted to `amqp:not-found`, which then maps to `ServiceCommunicationError`, and I need to verify whether that's actually included in the retryable errors list.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8558213Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8558837Z ✓ $ grep -n "retryableErrors\|ENOTFOUND\|ServiceCommunicationError" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts | head -30
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8559447Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8559708Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [557, 600])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8560013Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8560139Z ◆ Now I understand the full picture:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8560375Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8560700Z   1. `ENOTFOUND` maps to `amqp:not-found` → `ServiceCommunicationError`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8561366Z   2. `ServiceCommunicationError` is NOT in the retryable errors list (only `MessagingError` is listed there, not specific error codes)
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8562094Z   3. Wait, actually `MessagingError` IS in the retryable errors, but it's the error name, not code
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8562521Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8562754Z   Actually wait, let me re-read the retry function logic:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8563051Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8563208Z   ```typescript
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8563571Z   if ((errors[errors?.length - 1] as MessagingError).retryable && totalNumberOfAttempts > i) {
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8564009Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8564156Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8564304Z   ```
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8564450Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8565530Z   So it checks if the error is retryable. The translated error from `ENOTFOUND` goes through `isSystemError` and then gets mapped to `ServiceCommunicationError`, which isn't in the retryable list, so `error.retryable` gets set to false. That means the retry loop breaks after the first attempt and throws only one error.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8566896Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8567644Z   But wait, if there's only one error in the array, the code checks `if (errors.length === 1)` and throws that single error directly instead of wrapping it in an AggregateError. So why is an AggregateError being thrown at all?
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8568589Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8568734Z   ```
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8568905Z   if (errors.length === 1) {
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8569126Z     throw errors[0];
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8569312Z   } else {
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8569498Z     throw new AggregateError(errors);
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8569727Z   }
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8569879Z   ```
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8570028Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8572182Z   The test is showing 4 errors inside the AggregateError, which suggests multiple failures are happening. I'm wondering if this could be related to Node.js's DNS resolution behavior in newer versions—when hostname resolution fails, Node.js might return an AggregateError combining IPv4 and IPv6 lookup failures. If `rhea-promise` is receiving an AggregateError from the network layer and the `translate()` function doesn't properly handle it, that could explain why it's being thrown as-is instead of being unwrapped.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8573979Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8574494Z ✓ $ grep -n "isSystemError\|SystemError" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts | head -20
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8575033Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8575302Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [604, 665])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8575625Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8575745Z ◆ Now I understand the root cause! 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8576208Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8577396Z   `isSystemError` checks for `["code", "syscall", "errno"]` properties. An `AggregateError` from Node.js DNS resolution may not have these properties directly on it - it's an aggregation of multiple errors. The individual errors within the `AggregateError.errors[]` array would have these properties, but the `AggregateError` itself might not.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8578627Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8579682Z   So when Node.js DNS resolution fails with an `AggregateError` (which happens in newer versions of Node when both IPv4 and IPv6 lookups fail), the `translate()` function doesn't recognize it as a system error, so it just returns it as-is without wrapping it in a `MessagingError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8580693Z    
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8581995Z   Then in the retry loop, the `AggregateError` gets treated as a regular error - since it doesn't have the `retryable` property that a `MessagingError` would have, the loop breaks immediately and throws the raw `AggregateError` instead of properly handling it. The test shows this error contains multiple inner errors, which suggests the DNS lookup failed for multiple address families.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8583352Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8584104Z   Looking at the `translate()` function, it doesn't actually handle `AggregateError` at all - it checks for various error types but none of them match, so the `AggregateError` passes through untouched and gets wrapped as-is.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8584924Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8586224Z   Then in the retry logic, since the raw `AggregateError` doesn't have a `.retryable` property, it's treated as non-retryable, the loop breaks immediately, and the original `AggregateError` with all 4 nested errors gets thrown back to the test. The fix would be to add proper `AggregateError` handling in the `translate()` function.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8587405Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8588168Z   But wait—the test shows "0 new, 9 recurring," meaning this has been failing consistently for a while, not a recent regression. Let me check what Node.js version is actually running here.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8588870Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8589725Z   The runner is `ubuntu_22x_node`, which likely means Ubuntu 22.04 with Node.js 22.x or similar. In Node.js 22+, DNS resolution failures now throw `AggregateError` instead of the traditional `ENOTFOUND` error. Th... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3978 chars]
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8590588Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8591141Z ✓ $ grep -n "maxRetries\|retryOptions\|defaultRetry" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/retry.ts | head -20
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8591817Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8592371Z ✓ $ grep -n "defaultMaxRetries" /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js/sdk/core/core-amqp/src/util/constants.ts 2>/dev/null | head -5
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8592908Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8593501Z ◆ Now it makes sense! The default max retries is 3, so there's 1 initial attempt + 3 retries = 4 total attempts. But the retry logic says `break` if `!retryable`. However...
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8594138Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8594313Z   Wait, let me re-read the retry code flow:
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8594566Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8594839Z   1. Initial attempt fails with `AggregateError` from Node.js DNS resolution
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8595507Z   2. `translate(AggregateError)` → returns the `AggregateError` as-is (since it doesn't match any special cases)
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8596349Z   3. `errors.push(AggregateError)` → `errors.length === 1`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8596644Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8596794Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8597084Z   4. The error doesn't have a `retryable` property, so it's treated as `false`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8597517Z   5. Since it's not retryable, the loop breaks immediately
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8597936Z   6. With only one error collected, it throws that raw `AggregateError` directly
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8598301Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8598722Z   So the test is seeing the original Node.js `AggregateError` with its 4 inner DNS errors, not a wrapped `MessagingError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8599216Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8600212Z   The solution is to update the `translate()` function in `core-amqp/src/errors.ts` to recognize and unwrap `AggregateError` by recursively translating its first inner error. I'll add this check before the existing `isSystemError` logic and handle the case where the `AggregateError` is empty.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8601279Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8606167Z   Handling the AggregateError case... So the `AggregateError` contains multiple DNS resolution failures, and by extracting the first error, I'd get the `ENOTFOUND` code that the test expects. But I'm noticing the test also references `SystemErrorConditionMapper.ENOTFOUND`, which suggests there's a mapping I need to check to ensure the error code is being translated correctly. I'm tracing through the error mapping logic to see how the error code gets set on the MessagingError object. The `errorType` variable is being populated through the mapper chain, but I need to check whether `error.code` is actually being assigned from the original condition or if it's derived from somewhere else in the MessagingError constructor.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8609579Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8609927Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [455, 560])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8610265Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8610626Z ◆ Now I understand the full picture! When `translate()` is called with a system error (like `ENOTFOUND`):
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8611095Z   1. `isSystemError` returns true
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8611421Z   2. `const condition = errObj.code` → `"ENOTFOUND"`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8612203Z   3. `const error = new MessagingError(description, errObj)` → copies all system error properties including `code: "ENOTFOUND"` into the `MessagingError`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8613008Z   4. `errorType = ConditionErrorNameMapper["amqp:not-found"] = "ServiceCommunicationError"`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8613954Z   5. `if (retryableErrors.indexOf(errorType) === -1) { error.retryable = false; }` → `ServiceCommunicationError` not in retryableErrors → `error.retryable = false`
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8614589Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8614741Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8615153Z   6. The resulting `MessagingError` has `code = "ENOTFOUND"` and `retryable = false`, which matches the test expectation.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8615637Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8616997Z   However, there's a compatibility issue with newer Node.js versions that throw `AggregateError` for DNS resolution failures. Since `AggregateError` lacks the `code`, `syscall`, and `errno` properties that `isSystemError` checks for, it fails the system error detection and the function doesn't handle it properly.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8618327Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8618894Z   The solution is to update the `translate()` function to detect `AggregateError` and recursively translate its first inner error instead of returning it as-is.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8619535Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8622014Z   Writing the fix... The inner error surfaces as an `ENOTFOUND` system error that maps to `MessagingError` with the correct code. The failing test is in the real test suite, not snippets, and it's running in the CI pipeline. I've already confirmed that `ENOTFOUND` has the proper mapping in `SystemErrorConditionMapper`, so I can now document this issue on GitHub with all the details. So if the fix works, there's just one attempt with one error, and the `MessagingError` gets thrown directly without wrapping in an `AggregateError`. Looking at the CI pipeline failures, the eventhub test failures are all recurring issues marked as non-new, and the rest appear to be infrastructure problems like OIDC timeouts and manifest generation failures.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8624522Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8624936Z   Since these eventhub failures are pre-existing and undocumented, I ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2041 chars]

2026-04-21T10:44:28.8635442Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8635721Z ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure..., view_range: [676, 698])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8636261Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8636605Z ✓ safeoutputs-create_issue(title: CI test failures on main: @azure/event-h..., body: ## Summary
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8636966Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8637079Z CI analysis of commit [`5020..., labels: [bug])
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8637271Z 
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8637371Z ◆ **Summary:**
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8637539Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8638000Z   Found **1 package** with real unit test failures on the CI (playback) pipeline: **`@azure/event-hubs`** with 9 recurring failing tests.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8638544Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8640183Z   **Root cause:** In Node.js 22+, DNS resolution failures are returned as `AggregateError` (combining multiple lookup failures) rather than a plain system error. The `translate()` function in `@azure/core-amqp` doesn't handle `AggregateError`, so it passes through untranslated instead of being wrapped in a `MessagingError`. Tests that connect to a non-existent namespace (`sb://a`) expect a `MessagingError` with `code: "ENOTFOUND"` but receive the raw `AggregateError`.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8641958Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8642458Z   **Suggested fix:** Add an `AggregateError` guard in `sdk/core/core-amqp/src/errors.ts#translate()` to recursively translate the first inner error.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8643032Z   
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8643606Z   All other CI failures (browser runner exits, missing artifact paths, OIDC token timeouts, broken external links) had **0 test failures** and are infrastructure-only.
2026-04-21T10:44:28.8644195Z

The automated [Analyze CI Test Failures](.github/workflows/fix-test-failures.md) workflow uses this issue to skip known failures and avoid filing duplicate issues.\\n\\n## Known Failures\\n\\n_No known CI (playback) failures at this time._\\n\\n\\u0026gt; **Note:** This list only tracks failures from **CI pipelines** (`ci.yml`, playback mode). Live-test pipeline failures (`tests.yml`) are excluded — the workflow filters them out by check-run name.\\n\\n## Maintenance Guidelines\\n\\n- **Adding entries:** When a CI failure is confirmed to be a pre-existing infrastructure/service issue (not a code bug), add it here with the package name, test pattern, error summary, root cause, and a tracking issue link.\\n- **Removing entries:** When the underlying issue is fixed and CI is green again, remove the entry and close the corresponding tracking issue.\\n- **Format:** Each entry must include the **package name** (as it appears in check-run names) so the workflow can match it.\\n- **Review cadence:** Review this list monthly to check if any known failures have been resolved.\\n\",\"state\":\"open\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-js/issues/37864\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"deyaaeldeen\",\"id\":6074665,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/deyaaeldeen\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6074665?v=4\"},\"author_association\":\"MEMBER\",\"labels\":[\"test-reliability\"],\"reactions\":{\"total_count\":0,\"+1\":0,\"-1\":0,\"laugh\":0,\"confused\":0,\"heart\":0,\"hooray\":0,\"rocket\":0,\"eyes\":0},\"created_at\":\"2026-03-27T04:32:04Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-03-27T16:33:26Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}

2026-04-21T10:44:29.1120298Z [36;1m# AWF runs with sudo, creating files owned by root[0m
2026-04-21T10:44:29.1120504Z [36;1msudo chmod -R a+r /tmp/gh-aw/sandbox/firewall/logs 2>/dev/null || true[0m

2026-04-21T10:44:29.1120954Z [36;1mif command -v awf &> /dev/null; then[0m
